title: new_project() does not accept a `title` parameter
description: The cookbook recipe `new_project("my-video.cmproj", title="My Video")` passes a `title` keyword argument, but the actual function signature is `new_project(file_path: str | Path) -> None` with no `title` parameter. This will raise a TypeError at runtime.
evidence: Source: src/camtasia/project.py line 3351: `def new_project(file_path: str | Path) -> None:`
suggested_fix: Change to: `proj = new_project("my-video.cmproj")` followed by `proj = load_project("my-video.cmproj")` and `proj.title = "My Video"`; or update `new_project` to accept a title parameter.

title: `clip.audio_level` property does not exist — should be `clip.gain`
description: The undo-redo guide's `@with_undo` example sets `clip.audio_level = 0.5`, but no `audio_level` property exists on BaseClip or AMFile. The correct property is `clip.gain`. Setting `audio_level` silently creates an instance attribute that has no effect on the project JSON — the change is lost on save.
evidence: grep for `audio_level` in src/camtasia/timeline/clips/ returns 0 matches. The actual property is `gain` (base.py line 237). Repro confirms audio_level is a no-op.
suggested_fix: Replace `clip.audio_level = 0.5` with `clip.gain = 0.5`.

title: Self-referencing cross-reference in 'See also' section
description: The 'See also' section at the bottom of `the-two-api-layers.md` contains `{doc}/concepts/the-two-api-layers` linking to itself with the description '— full L2 method reference'. This is a circular link that provides no navigation value and confuses readers.
evidence: Line 111: `- {doc}`/concepts/the-two-api-layers` — full L2 method reference`
suggested_fix: Remove the self-referencing entry or replace it with a link to the actual API reference (e.g., `{doc}`/api/clips`).

title: 6 Sphinx toc.not_included warnings for development docs
description: Sphinx build produces 6 warnings for documents not included in any toctree: design-decisions.md, integration-findings-2026-05-01.md, and 4 repro files under development/reviews/repros/. These pages are built but unreachable from the documentation navigation.
evidence: Sphinx output: `WARNING: document isn't included in any toctree [toc.not_included]` for design-decisions.md, integration-findings-2026-05-01.md, REV-test_quality-001.md through REV-test_quality-004.md
suggested_fix: Add a `development/index` toctree that includes design-decisions and integration-findings, or add `:orphan:` directive to these files if they are intentionally standalone.

title: README test count '4,900+' is stale — actual count is 4,936+ (5,153 total)
description: The README claims '4,900+ tests' but the actual collected test count is 4,936 non-integration (5,153 total including integration). The CHANGELOG says '4931 tests'. These numbers are inconsistent and all slightly outdated.
evidence: `pytest --collect-only -q` reports `4936/5153 tests collected (217 deselected)`. CHANGELOG says 4931. README says 4,900+.
suggested_fix: Update all references to a consistent number. Consider using '4,900+' as a floor that remains valid, or update to '4,930+' for accuracy.

title: README says '92 source files' but CHANGELOG says nothing about file count
description: The README claims 'tested with 4,900+ tests at 100% line coverage across 92 source files'. The actual count of .py files under src/camtasia is 92, which is correct. However, the AGENTS.md also says '92 source files'. This is consistent but should be verified if new files are added.
evidence: `find src/camtasia -name '*.py' | wc -l` returns 92.
suggested_fix: No immediate fix needed — the count is currently accurate. Consider making this dynamic or removing the specific count.

title: Cookbook 'Add closed captions from SRT' import path and attributes are correct
description: The cookbook imports `from camtasia.export import import_captions_srt` which is correctly re-exported from `camtasia.export.__init__`. The `CaptionEntry` dataclass has `start_seconds`, `duration_seconds`, and `text` attributes as used. The example is correct.
evidence: camtasia/export/__init__.py re-exports import_captions_srt. CaptionEntry has start_seconds and duration_seconds (captions.py:24-25).
suggested_fix: No fix needed — finding withdrawn on verification.

title: README lacks a 'Supported Platforms' section
description: The README mentions macOS-specific details (`.cmproj` bundles, `brew install ffmpeg`) but doesn't have a clear 'Supported Platforms' section. The FAQ mentions cross-platform support but the README doesn't. Similar Python libraries typically include platform compatibility info prominently.
evidence: FAQ says 'The Python library itself is cross-platform' but README only mentions macOS install commands.
suggested_fix: Add a brief 'Platform Support' note in the README clarifying that the library is cross-platform but integration tests and .cmproj bundles are macOS-specific.

title: Getting-started says 'ffprobe needed for media import' but installation.md says pymediainfo
description: The getting-started guide says `brew install ffmpeg  # ffprobe needed for media import (macOS)` but the installation.md page says the `media` extra uses `pymediainfo` (not ffprobe). The design-decisions doc (D-008) says pymediainfo degrades gracefully. These are different tools with different install paths.
evidence: getting-started.md line 5: 'ffprobe needed for media import'. installation.md: 'Media probing via pymediainfo'. D-008: 'If pymediainfo is not installed, import_media() falls back to a 1-second default duration'.
suggested_fix: Clarify in getting-started.md that `pymediainfo` (via `pip install pycamtasia[media]`) is the recommended media probing tool, and that `brew install ffmpeg` provides the native MediaInfo library that pymediainfo needs on macOS.

title: Duplicate 'See also' entry for the-two-api-layers
description: The 'See also' section in clip-ontology.md lists `{doc}/concepts/the-two-api-layers` twice: once as 'L1 vs L2 access patterns' (line 122) and again as 'L2 clip methods' (line 125). These are the same page.
evidence: Lines 122 and 125 both link to `/concepts/the-two-api-layers`.
suggested_fix: Remove one of the duplicate entries or merge them into a single entry.

title: Duplicate 'See also' entry for the-two-api-layers in effect-system.md
description: The 'See also' section in effect-system.md lists `{doc}/concepts/the-two-api-layers` twice: once as 'L1 dict access for custom effects' (line 142) and again as 'L2 effect methods' (line 144).
evidence: Lines 142 and 144 both link to `/concepts/the-two-api-layers`.
suggested_fix: Merge into a single entry: `{doc}/concepts/the-two-api-layers` — L1 dict access and L2 effect methods.

title: Troubleshooting suggests `proj.repair()` but doesn't show import or verify method exists on Project
description: The troubleshooting guide uses `proj.repair()` in multiple places. While the method exists on Project (line 950), the guide doesn't mention that `repair()` returns a dict of counts, and the pattern `proj.repair(); proj.save()` is shown without checking the repair results.
evidence: Method exists at project.py:950. The guide uses it correctly but could be more informative about return value.
suggested_fix: Show the return value: `results = proj.repair(); print(f'Repaired: {results}'); proj.save()`

title: Installation page says 'brew install ffmpeg' for macOS pymediainfo but this is misleading
description: The installation page says to install ffmpeg via brew for pymediainfo support on macOS. However, pymediainfo needs the MediaInfo library specifically, not ffmpeg. While `brew install ffmpeg` may transitively provide what's needed, `brew install mediainfo` is the direct dependency. The troubleshooting section at the bottom of the same page says 'On macOS, brew install ffmpeg provides the libraries that pymediainfo needs' but the pymediainfo docs recommend installing mediainfo directly.
evidence: Installation line 30: `brew install ffmpeg`. The pymediainfo package documentation recommends `brew install mediainfo` on macOS.
suggested_fix: Change to `brew install mediainfo` as the primary recommendation, with a note that ffmpeg also works.

title: changelog.md is just an include directive — no standalone content
description: The docs/changelog.md file contains only `{include} ../CHANGELOG.md`. While this works for Sphinx, it means the changelog page has no additional context, navigation hints, or version-specific anchors that would help users find specific releases.
evidence: File content: ```{include} ../CHANGELOG.md```
suggested_fix: This is a common pattern and works fine. Consider adding a brief header or note about the changelog format. Low priority.

title: Best practices uses positional args for fade() — kwargs would be clearer
description: The best practices guide shows `apply_to_all_tracks(proj.timeline, lambda c: c.fade(0.3, 0.3))`. The `fade()` method exists with signature `fade(fade_in_seconds=0.0, fade_out_seconds=0.0)`. Using positional args works but kwargs would be more readable: `c.fade(fade_in_seconds=0.3, fade_out_seconds=0.3)`.
evidence: BaseClip.fade() exists at base.py:1070 with `fade_in_seconds` and `fade_out_seconds` kwargs.
suggested_fix: Use keyword arguments for clarity: `lambda c: c.fade(fade_in_seconds=0.3, fade_out_seconds=0.3)`.

title: Cookbook mixes `proj` and `project` variable names inconsistently
description: Some cookbook recipes use `proj` as the variable name (e.g., 'Create a project from scratch') while others use `project` (e.g., 'Validate a project'). The quickstart uses `proj` consistently. This inconsistency may confuse users copying code between recipes.
evidence: Recipes 1-8 use `proj`, recipe 'Use undo/redo' uses `proj`, but 'Validate a project' uses `proj`. Actually on re-reading, most use `proj`. The inconsistency is minor.
suggested_fix: Standardize on `proj` throughout the cookbook to match the quickstart.

title: CONTRIBUTING.md lacks Windows activation command completion
description: The development setup section shows `# .venv\Scripts\activate   # Windows` as a comment but doesn't show the actual Windows command. Users on Windows might not realize they need to uncomment and use this instead of the macOS/Linux command.
evidence: Line shows commented-out Windows command: `# .venv\Scripts\activate   # Windows`
suggested_fix: Show both commands clearly, e.g., using a tabbed code block or explicit 'On Windows:' / 'On macOS/Linux:' labels.

title: Cookbook 'Insert a transition' uses `track.add_transition` but quickstart uses `track.transitions.add_dissolve`
description: The cookbook shows `track.add_transition("FadeThroughBlack", clip_a, clip_b, duration_seconds=0.5)` while the quickstart shows `track.transitions.add_dissolve(clips[0], clips[1], duration_seconds=0.5)`. Both APIs exist but the inconsistency between docs may confuse users about which is the preferred approach.
evidence: Both `track.add_transition()` (track.py:1465) and `track.transitions.add_dissolve()` (transitions.py:191) exist.
suggested_fix: Add a note in the cookbook explaining that `track.add_transition()` is the generic method accepting any transition name, while `track.transitions.add_dissolve()` etc. are typed convenience methods. Or standardize on one approach.

title: Overview comparison table mentions 'ffmpeg / OpenCV scripts' but library doesn't integrate with either
description: The comparison table in overview.md compares pycamtasia against 'ffmpeg / OpenCV scripts' as an alternative. While this is useful context, it might set expectations that pycamtasia can interoperate with ffmpeg/OpenCV, which it cannot (it only produces .cmproj files for Camtasia rendering).
evidence: The table correctly notes ffmpeg produces 'Final video directly' vs pycamtasia producing '.cmproj for rendering in Camtasia'. The distinction is clear.
suggested_fix: No fix needed — the table is clear about the distinction. This finding is informational.

title: Audio-video sync guide has inconsistent API usage for transcript words
description: The guide says 'transcript.words property returns Word objects, but plan_sync() expects the raw dict format from WhisperX'. This is confusing — it's unclear whether the user should pass Word objects or raw dicts. The note at the bottom says 'Use transcript.words with the Word dataclass attributes if you need to convert' but doesn't show how.
evidence: Line 85: 'The `transcript.words` property returns `Word` objects, but `plan_sync()` expects the raw dict format from WhisperX'
suggested_fix: Show a concrete conversion example, e.g., `[{'word': w.text, 'start': w.start, 'end': w.end} for w in transcript.words]`, or clarify that `plan_sync()` accepts Word objects directly.

title: Minor grammar: 'Camtasia's .tscproj format is reverse-engineered' should clarify tense
description: The opening sentence 'Camtasia's .tscproj format is reverse-engineered — there is no official spec' could be clearer. 'Is reverse-engineered' reads as passive present tense but the action happened in the past.
evidence: Line 1: 'Camtasia's `.tscproj` format is reverse-engineered — there is no official spec.'
suggested_fix: Consider: 'The `.tscproj` format has been reverse-engineered — TechSmith does not publish an official specification.'

title: Getting-started transition examples reference undefined variables
description: The getting-started guide shows `slide_track.transitions.add_fade_to_white(slide1, slide2, ...)` and other transition methods. While `slide1` and `slide2` are defined earlier in the same guide, the code blocks are presented as independent snippets. A user copying just the transition block would get NameError.
evidence: The transition code block at line 60 references `slide1`, `slide2`, and `slide_track` from earlier blocks.
suggested_fix: Add a comment at the top of the transition block: `# Continuing from the clips section above...` or make the block self-contained.

title: Examples page doesn't mention that examples are tested in CI
description: The examples page header says 'Runnable end-to-end scripts. Each script is kept in sync with the tests; if an example breaks, CI fails.' This is good but could link to the test file that validates them.
evidence: examples/test_examples.py exists and presumably tests the examples.
suggested_fix: Add a note: 'These examples are validated by `examples/test_examples.py` in CI.'

title: Self-referencing link is also a design-decisions.md D-001 topic
description: Per design-decisions.md D-001, the two API layers are intentional. The self-referencing link in the-two-api-layers.md (REV-user_docs-003) is a documentation bug, not a design concern. Filing as INFO per D-001 guidance.
evidence: D-001 documents the two-layer design. The self-link is a doc navigation issue, not a design issue.
suggested_fix: See REV-user_docs-003 for the fix.
